text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Susan  
University College Hospital  
National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery, London WC1N 3BG  

Dear Dr Susan,  

William Harris, DOB: 15-02-1965, Hospital No: H563829 NHS No. 9273645810  
10 Meadow Park, London, EC1V 9BT 

Summary/ Diagnoses: Epilepsy with prior head injury in adolescence/long-standing mood and sleep disturbance/past vitamin D insufficiency/falls risk assessment completed in primary care/variable community pharmacy supply record with intermittent historical adherence concerns/currently living independently with weekly carer check-ins. 

Medication: 
- Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily. 
- Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily (uptitrated gradually over the past 6 weeks). 
- Cholecalciferol 1000 IU daily, Omeprazole 20 mg daily, Sertraline 50 mg each morning. 

I reviewed William Harris in clinic today, accompanied by his sister who provides collateral information on a weekly basis. He reported that work-related stress and poor sleep have been prominent since July. He uses a pill organiser. There is a documented history from the community pharmacy of past variability in supply, and both he and his sister acknowledge that at those times adherence was affected; he states he has now implemented reminders and has not missed doses in the last month. He reports no recent head injuries, no new focal neurological symptoms, and no use of alcohol or recreational drugs. 

Seizures: Over the past few months, tonic seizures have increased in frequency to multiple per day, in contrast to the earlier pattern of one seizure every month or two. Episodes are brief (reported 10–20 seconds), with a stiffening posture and occasional backward falls. Post-event fatigue lasts up to 30 minutes. He has not had any prolonged events requiring emergency care. His sister has started recording events in a phone diary; over the last 14 days she has logged daily clusters on 9 days, with quieter mornings and more events in the late afternoon. There is no clear relation to meals. He denies missed doses during this period but did report two pharmacy-delayed collections in late August; he believes he had tablets to cover those days from a previous box. 

Examination: Alert and oriented, speech fluent, cranial nerves intact, tone and power normal in all limbs, coordination intact, gait steady with normal base, Romberg negative. No tongue injury today; minor ecchymoses on right forearm consistent with recent falls. BP 128/78, pulse 74 regular, weight stable. 

Investigations: 
- Bloods arranged via GP last month reportedly normal; I have requested copies. Today I requested U&E, LFT, FBC, CRP, vitamin D, and lamotrigine level (for reference) via the hospital phlebotomy service. 
- MRI brain from 2022 unchanged from 2017 (report reviewed). 
- 24-hour ambulatory EEG requested to capture habitual events and diurnal pattern. 

Plan: 
- Continue Levetiracetam 1500 mg twice daily. 
- Continue Lamotrigine 100 mg twice daily pending ambulatory EEG and blood results; dosing to be reviewed in multidisciplinary meeting once data available. 
- Safety: discussed fall precautions, shower rather than bath, and use of soft headrest on favourite recliner given frequent afternoon events. His sister will supervise meal preparation when feasible. 
- Adherence/supply: pharmacy has been asked to provide advance notice of any stock issues and to align all repeat medications to a single collection date; patient will use a monitored dosage system for the next 8 weeks. 
- Diary: continue detailed seizure diary noting time of day, potential triggers, and any missed or delayed doses. 
- Driving: standard advice reiterated; he is not driving. 
- Follow-up: nurse specialist phone review in 3 weeks to assess frequency and tolerability; clinic review in 8–10 weeks or sooner if events escalate or injuries occur. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the past few months, tonic seizures have increased in frequency to multiple per day

Additionally, the sister's diary over the last 14 days recorded daily clusters on 9 days, indicating frequent daily events but without a precise count per day. The highest explicit frequency is 'multiple per day.' No exact numeric value is provided, so 'multiple' is appropriate per the format rules.
